english: W., a spectator, a visitor; ltád-mo-mkan (ltad mo mkhan), ltád-mo stón-pa (ltad mo ston pa) a showman, actor, mimic etc. — gróṅ-yul-gyi ltád-mo ma dran żiṅ (grong yul gyi ltad mo ma dran zhing) Mil. forget the scenes of village life!

pf. bltabs (bltabs), fut. bltab (bltab), imp. ltob (ltob) (W. *ltabs toṅ* (*ltabs tong*)), to fold or gather up, to lay or put together, *kyaṅ-tab* (*kyang tab*), nyi-tab tab-cè* (*nyi tab tab ce*) W. to fold single, to fold double; ysum-ltab byéd-pa (gsum ltab byed pa) to fold or bend together threefold, e.g. a corpse previous to cremation; ltáb-ma (ltab ma) Cs. a fold, crease plait; ltab-gri (ltab gri) a clasp knife

pf. bltams (bltams), fut. bltam (bltam), 1. to be full, also ytáms-pa (gtams pa). — 2. resp. to be born, skyéd-pai yab daṅ bltáms-pai yum (skyed pai yab dang bltams pai yum) the father by whom one is begotten, and the mother by whom one is born Pth.

1. also bltar (bltar), supine of ltá-ba (lta ba), in order to see; bltár-ruṅ-ba (bltar rung ba) visible; Sch.: 'pleasing to the eye'; gaṅ ltár-na yaṅ (gang ltar na yang), ći ltár-na yaṅ (ci ltar na yang), be that as it may Glr. — 2. postp. c. a., like, as, after the manner of, ri-ltar (ri ltar) like a mountain; pyag byéd-pa ltar byéd-pa (phyag byed pa ltar byed pa) to make a saluting gesture Glr.; ṅo-śes ruṅ mi śés-pa ltar byas (ngo shes rung mi shes pa ltar byas) although they knew . . . , they affected not to know . . . Mil.; bral mi pód-pa ltar yód-na yaṅ (bral mi phod pa ltar yod na yang) being like one that cannot part with, = being scarcely able to part with, Glr.; ltar snáṅ-ba (ltar snang ba) to appear like, hence prob. ltar-snáṅ (ltar snang) appearance, similarity Sch., (Lex. w.e.); lúṅ-bstan-pa ltar (lung bstan pa ltar) (to do a thing) in conformity with a prediction Tar.; also ltár-na (ltar na), and ltár-du (ltar du), mi-lo ltár-na . . . yod (mi lo ltar na . . . yod) computed by human or terrestrial years it amounts to . . . Thgy.; bód-rnams ltár-na (bod rnams ltar na) according to Tibetan (sources) Tar.; ći-ltar(-na) (ci ltar (-na)) how? in what manner or way? ći-ltar (ci ltar) also serves to paraphrase the English 'so that' e.g. 'he played so that all were enraptured' is thus expressed: he played — how did he play? — all were enraptured; ji-ltar(-na) (ji ltar (-na)) as ji-lta ji-ltar . . . dé-lta dé-ltar (ji lta ji ltar . . . de lta de ltar) Sambh. even as . . . so; di-ltar (di ltar), dé-ltar(-na) (de ltar (-na)) so, thus, in that manner; di-ltar mi rgan kyod (di ltar mi rgan khyod) such an old fellow as you are; frq. also in referring to the words of others, where we use 'that': dé-ltar bdén-na (de ltar bden na) if that is true.
Lex., Cs.: of a liquid nature, as an embryo first in the womb.

prognostic, omen, more distinctive sná-ltas (sna ltas); miraculous sign, miracle, prodigy, more accurately: ṅo-mtsár-bai ltas (ngo mtshar bai ltas); bkra-śis-pai ltas (bkra shis pai ltas) a propitious omen; rmi-ltas bzáṅ-po (rmi ltas bzang po) a good sign in a dream Pth.; dgé-ltas (dge ltas) a favourable sign; ṅán-ltas (ngan ltas), or ltas-ṅán (ltas ngan) a bad sign Dzl.; ltás-mkan (ltas mkhan) a soothsayer, fortune-teller; ltas stón-pa (ltas ston pa) to soothsay Cs.
C. a person of small stature, perh. a corruption of lte-túṅ (lte tung).

1. vb., pf. lhuṅ (lhung), to fall, to fall off, down, into; fig.: mtó-ba de yaṅ mtar lhúṅ-ṅo (mtho ba de yang mthar lhung ngo) what is high will finally fall down Dzl.; more esp. to fall into sin, to commit sin, hence nyes-ltuṅ (nyes ltung) an actual sin, a sinful deed, ltúṅ-byed (ltung byed) a transgression, crime; also ṅán-soṅ-du (ngan song du) (v. gró-ba I, 5 (gro ba I, 5)), or dmyál-bar (dmyal bar) to fall into damnation. — 2. sbst. the fall, esp. the moral fall, ltúṅ-bas gós-pa (ltung bas gos pa) polluted by sin; ltúṅ-ba bśágs-pa (ltung ba bshags pa) confession of sin.

1. navel-string, umbilical cord, gćód-pa (gcod pa) to cut it Med. — 2. navel, lté-bai kúṅ(bu) (lte bai khung (bu)) Lt. id.; glá-bai lté-ba (gla bai lte ba) musk-bag. — 3. the middle of a thing, centre, dkyil-kor-gyi (dkyil khor gyi) of a circle; mu-kyúd ysum-gyi lté-bar (mu khyud gsum gyi lte bar) in the middle of three (concentric) circles Lt.; raṅ-tág-gi lté-ba (rang thag gi lte ba) the axle-tree of a water-wheel Glr.; sai lté-ba (sai lte ba) the centre of the earth, in the opinion of the natives: Tibet; also cognomen of several fabulous kings of Tibet Köpp. II., 52. — lté-ba yzuṅ-ráṅ (lte ba gzung rang) Lhasa, or, in a more special sense, the palace of the Dalai Lama. — lte-túg (lte tug) W. = *tig-túṅ* (*tig tung*) C.
1. v. ltaṅ (ltang). — 2. lteṅ-rgyás (lteng rgyas) n. of a Buddha.
